title: Wine Bottle Guide
subtitle: Shapes, sizes, and why they matter

shapes.title: Regional Bottle Shapes
shapes.description: Wine bottle shapes evolved regionally, each designed for practical reasons that became tradition. The shape often hints at what's inside.

bottles.name: Bordeaux
bottles.practical_reason: High shoulders catch sediment when pouring aged wines

bottles.name: Burgundy
bottles.shape: Sloping shoulders, wider body, gentle curves
bottles.color: Dark green (red), pale green or clear (white)
bottles.practical_reason: Pinot Noir produces less sediment; sloped shoulders aid stacking

bottles.name: Alsace / Mosel
bottles.practical_reason: Designed for river transport; tall shape fit more bottles in boats

bottles.name: Champagne
bottles.shape: Heavy, thick glass, deep punt, sloping shoulders
bottles.practical_reason: Thick glass withstands 6 atmospheres of pressure; deep punt adds strength

bottles.name: Rhone
bottles.shape: Similar to Burgundy but with embossed coat of arms
bottles.practical_reason: Embossed papal keys or coat of arms indicate regional authenticity

bottles.name: Port / Sherry
bottles.shape: Bulbous neck (Port) or straight (Sherry), pronounced shoulders
bottles.practical_reason: Dark glass protects from light; bulbous neck catches heavy sediment

bottles.name: Provence Rose
bottles.practical_reason: Clear glass displays the pale salmon color; distinctive shape aids branding

bottles.name: Bocksbeutel
bottles.practical_reason: Protected designation; distinctive shape prevents counterfeiting

sizes.title: Bottle Sizes
sizes.description: Larger formats age more gracefully. Smaller bottles age faster. The relationship between wine volume and oxygen exposure through the cork creates different aging trajectories.
sizes.intro_note: Named after biblical kings and figures, large format bottles are prized for aging and celebration.

sizes.name: Magnum
sizes.aging_impact: Ideal for aging; slower, more graceful development

sizes.name: Jeroboam
sizes.note: Same size as Double Magnum but term used for Champagne

sizes.name: Rehoboam
sizes.named_after: King of Judah, son of Solomon

sizes.name: Imperial
sizes.named_after: Oldest person in the Bible (969 years)

sizes.name: Balthazar
sizes.named_after: One of the Three Wise Men

sizes.name: Melchior
sizes.named_after: One of the Three Wise Men

aging_explanation.title: Why Size Affects Aging
points: All bottles have roughly the same size cork
Larger bottles have less oxygen exposure per volume of wine
Slower oxidation means more gradual, complex development
Magnums are considered ideal: practical size with aging benefits
Very large formats are often filled from standard bottles, affecting consistency

science.title: Why Bottles Matter
science.description: Beyond tradition, bottle design serves practical purposes that affect how wine ages and tastes.

factors.explanation: Dark glass blocks UV rays that can cause 'lightstrike' - a fault that gives wine a wet cardboard or cabbage smell. White wines in clear glass are more vulnerable.
factors.detail: Brown glass blocks 90% of UV; green blocks 50%; clear blocks almost none

factors.explanation: High Bordeaux shoulders catch sediment when pouring. Sloped Burgundy shoulders don't need this since Pinot Noir produces less sediment.
factors.detail: Aged Bordeaux benefits most from high-shoulder design

factors.explanation: The indent in the bottom strengthens the bottle, especially important for sparkling wines under pressure. Also aids pouring grip and sediment collection.
factors.detail: Champagne bottles have deep punts; still wine punts are often shallow or absent

factors.explanation: Champagne bottles weigh 900g vs 400g for still wine. The thick glass contains 6 atmospheres of pressure - three times a car tire.
factors.detail: Lighter bottles are becoming common for environmental reasons in still wines

factors.explanation: Standard heights allow efficient case packing and cellar storage. The Alsace flute was designed for river transport efficiency.
factors.detail: Most wine racks are designed for Bordeaux-height bottles

lightstrike_note.title: Lightstrike
lightstrike_note.description: UV light triggers a chemical reaction with riboflavin and amino acids in wine, creating sulfur compounds. The result smells of wet cardboard, cooked cabbage, or burnt rubber. White wines and Champagne are most vulnerable. Store wine in darkness.

aging.title: Aging & Oxidation
aging.description: Wine ages through slow, controlled oxygen exposure. The bottle, cork, and storage conditions determine whether a wine develops complexity or falls apart.

oxygen_exchange.title: How Wine Breathes
oxygen_exchange.description: Cork allows roughly 1mg of oxygen per year to enter the bottle. This micro-oxygenation drives the slow chemical reactions that transform young wine into something more complex.

process.what_happens: Primary fruit flavors dominate. Tannins are grippy and separate from the fruit. Oxygen begins softening harsh edges.
Tannins polymerize and soften. Primary fruit evolves into secondary flavors. Oxygen integrates components.
Tertiary aromas emerge: earth, leather, mushroom, dried fruit. Color fades. Structure becomes silky.
Fruit fades entirely. Oxidation overtakes the wine. Structure collapses.

bottle_size_impact.title: Size & Aging Speed
bottle_size_impact.description: All corks allow similar oxygen ingress regardless of bottle size. Larger bottles have more wine per unit of oxygen, slowing development.

comparison.note: The reference point for aging curves
Half the aging speed of standard

closure_types.title: Closure & Oxygen
closure_types.description: Different closures allow different amounts of oxygen transmission.

closures.risk: TCA contamination (cork taint) in 2-5% of bottles
Lower TCA risk than natural cork
Possible reduction in very long aging
Inconsistent oxygen transmission; can dry out

oxidation_vs_reduction.title: Oxidation vs Reduction
oxidation_vs_reduction.description: Wine can suffer from too much oxygen (oxidation) or too little (reduction). Both are faults when extreme.

oxidation.title: Oxidation
oxidation.cause: Excessive oxygen exposure from poor storage, dried cork, or too long open
oxidation.prevention: Store on side, maintain humidity, proper cellar temperature

reduction.title: Reduction
reduction.cause: Too little oxygen, often from screwcaps or wines made with minimal sulfur handling
reduction.fix: Often blows off with decanting and air exposure

storage_factors.title: Storage Conditions
storage_factors.description: Proper storage is essential for aging. Poor conditions accelerate decline regardless of bottle quality.

conditions.impact: Every 10F above ideal doubles aging speed. Heat damage is irreversible.
Low humidity dries corks, allowing oxygen ingress. High humidity damages labels but not wine.
UV causes lightstrike. Even fluorescent lights can damage wine over time.
Vibration disturbs sediment and may accelerate chemical reactions.
Keeps cork moist. Screwcaps can store upright.

premature_oxidation.title: Premature Oxidation (Premox)
premature_oxidation.description: A controversial issue, particularly in white Burgundy from the late 1990s-2000s. Wines oxidize years before expected.
premature_oxidation.affected_wines: White Burgundy most famously, but seen in other regions
signs: Deep gold color in young wine
premature_oxidation.note: Premox remains debated. Some believe cork suppliers, others blame winemaking choices. Many top producers have since increased sulfur levels and improved cork selection.
